nativeRange.habitat: The Violet Goby inhabits brackish estuaries, muddy bays, river mouths, and coastal swamps, typically in mud-bottomed intertidal and subtidal zones with soft substrate. It occasionally moves into fully fresh water or open marine water, but spends most of its life in environments with measurable salinity.

decorPreferences: deep soft sand substrate (5+ cm) for partial burrowing
lengths of PVC pipe or plastic tubing as bolt-holes
smooth bogwood or driftwood for cover
low or no current — gentle filtration output only
dim or subdued lighting to reduce stress

diet.notes: The Violet Goby feeds by scooping mouthfuls of substrate and filtering out micro-organisms, algae, and detritus through its gill rakers — a method sometimes called filter-feeding or sifting. In captivity it generally accepts finely crushed or sinking micro-pellets, algae wafers, spirulina flakes, frozen baby brine shrimp, frozen bloodworms, frozen tubifex, and daphnia, provided the food is small enough to pass through the gill rakers. Despite its prominent teeth, these are used for scraping algae and biofilm from surfaces rather than for catching prey or threatening tankmates. Feeding once daily or in two small portions per day is typically recommended; placing food near hiding spots at lights-out takes advantage of the species' largely nocturnal activity pattern. Vegetable matter such as spirulina and algae wafers is considered important for long-term health.

goodWith: other large peaceful brackish fish (figure-eight puffers kept well-fed)
avoidWith: aggressive or nippy tankmates that will harass the slow-moving goby
fast competitive feeders that outcompete at feeding time
conspecifics in small tanks (territorial aggression toward own species)
small fish or invertebrates that may be accidentally ingested during filter-feeding

imageCandidates.notes: Cedricguppy - Loury Cedric; 4032x2272. Sharp, vivid aquarium shot showing the iridescent violet-blue head, mouth and pectoral fin with barred body - best display of the signature coloration.
Cedricguppy - Loury Cedric; 1024x577. Sharp side view showing the head plus a long stretch of the elongated eel-like body with iridescent violet-blue sheen and dark banding.
Kitty Kat Katarina; 1600x900. Full-length home-aquarium profile showing the complete eel-like body from head to tail; slightly hazy but adds whole-animal context.

summary: The Violet Goby is a large, eel-like brackish-water fish from Atlantic American estuaries, reaching up to 62 cm. Despite its formidable teeth and dragon-like appearance, it is a gentle filter-feeder. It is frequently sold as a freshwater fish, which leads to early decline; it requires brackish conditions (SG 1.005–1.012) for long-term health.
careNotes: The Violet Goby, also widely sold as Dragon Goby or Dragon Fish, is one of the largest gobies in the Western Atlantic, reaching up to 62 cm in the wild. Its elongated eel-like body, prominent teeth, and iridescent violet-silver colouring give it a striking, almost prehistoric look, but it is a docile, slow-moving filter-feeder that is poorly suited to the community freshwater aquaria in which it is most commonly sold. The single most important care requirement is brackish water: a specific gravity of SG 1.005–1.012 is broadly considered essential for long-term health. Fish maintained in pure freshwater may survive for months but tend to decline and die prematurely from osmotic stress and suppressed immunity. The species is native to Atlantic estuaries from South Carolina (USA) south to northern Brazil, inhabiting muddy bays, river mouths, and tidal swamps — environments with fluctuating but consistently measurable salinity. A minimum tank of 450 litres with a 120 cm footprint is generally recommended for a single adult. The tank should be tightly covered, as this species is considered a determined escape risk. Substrate should be at least 5 cm of fine soft sand; the goby spends much of its time partially buried or resting against the substrate, and coarse gravel can damage the belly and cause abrasions. Multiple hiding places are important: lengths of plastic pipe or PVC tubing, caves formed from smooth rock, and pieces of driftwood all work well. Filtration should be gentle, as strong current tends to stress these fish. Lighting should be dim or subdued, and a gradual dusk period in the photoperiod is beneficial for a species that is largely nocturnal. All foods offered should be small enough to be sieved through the gill rakers — micro-pellets, finely crushed flake, baby brine shrimp, bloodworms, and algae wafers are all suitable. Feeding near hiding spots at dusk helps ensure the goby gets its share. Housing with aggressive or fin-nipping species is generally inadvisable, as the Violet Goby's slow movement and long fins make it an easy target. Water quality should be kept stable; the species is commonly reported to be sensitive to ammonia and elevated nitrate. Weekly partial water changes of 10–15% are typically recommended. The similar-looking Gobioides peruanus is sometimes sold under the same trade names but reportedly tolerates freshwater better; the two species can be difficult to tell apart in a retail setting.
breedingNotes: No verified captive breeding of the Violet Goby has been documented. In the wild, spawning is presumed to occur in estuarine environments, possibly with some shift toward higher salinity, but the details remain poorly documented. The sexes can be distinguished by the genital papilla: males have a long, pointed one, while females have a short, blunt, yellowish one. No aquarium breeding protocol exists at this time.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Gobioides broussonnetii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 62.5 cm), temperature 22–26°C, pH 7.5–9.0, hardness 10–25°H, salinity SG 1.005–1.015, minimum tank 450 L (48×24×24 in), diet (algae/detritus/frozen foods), native range (S USA to Brazil including Caribbean), breeding (not achieved in captivity), and substrate/decor requirements.

additional.name: FishBase — Gobioides broussonnetii
additional.notes: Confirmed maximum size 55.3 cm TL, temperature range 14–32°C (optimal 23–28°C), salinity tolerance 0–25.5 ppt, native range (Charleston SC southward to Florida, Gulf of Mexico coast, Caribbean coast of Colombia east to Venezuela, Surinam, Guyana, French Guiana, and Brazil), taxonomy (family Gobiidae, order Gobiiformes), demersal/estuarine habitat. Trophic level 3.8.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Violet goby
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range, habitat (brackish estuaries/river mouths), diet (filamentous algae, micro-invertebrates scraped with teeth), sexual dimorphism (genital papilla), IUCN status (Least Concern), eel-like body morphology. Noted wild maximum 61 cm, captive specimens seldom exceed 38 cm.

additional.name: Aquarium Source — Dragon Fish Goby Care
additional.notes: Hobbyist-facing care guide. Confirmed salinity SG 1.006–1.008, temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.5–8.5, hardness 10–20 dKH, lifespan ~10 years, minimum tank 4 ft length for one specimen, feeding strategy (target-feed near hides), sensitivity to ammonia/nitrate, disease susceptibility (fin rot, columnaris), and partly-nocturnal behaviour.

additional.name: FishLore — Care Sheet for Dragon Goby (forum)
additional.notes: Community experience confirming brackish requirement (SG 1.005–1.010 recommended by experienced keepers), escape-risk warning, sand substrate necessity, pipe/tube hiding spots, and feeding approach for this micro-filter species.

fish.breedingNotes: No verified captive breeding has been recorded. Wild spawning details remain undocumented. Sexual dimorphism is visible through the genital papilla: males have a long, pointed one; females have a short, blunt, yellowish one. No aquarium breeding protocol has been established.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Sold as freshwater but requires BRACKISH (SG 1.005-1.012) for long-term health. Eel-like 60cm+ goby. Specialist large brackish setup.
